segments.s726-g1: 'ازآن پس' is a temporal adverbial phrase 'after that / from then on'. 'به آرام' is an adverbial phrase of manner ('in calm / peacefully') placed before the verb. 'بنشست' is the past tense (3rd singular) 'sat down', and 'شاه' is the subject 'the king'. In this poetic word order the time/manner phrases precede the verb, which is followed by the subject.
segments.s726-g2: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause meaning 'when'. 'برخاست' is the past tense 'rose/rose up' (3rd sg.), and 'بهرام' is the proper name (the subject). 'جنگی ز راه' is a compact noun phrase: 'جنگی' here means 'a warlike man / fighter / warrior' and 'ز راه' = 'from the road' (poetic 'ز' = 'from'). Note the verb of the subordinate ('برخاست') appears before its subject in this poetic order.
segments.s726-g3: ندید is the negative past 'did not see'. 'از بزرگان' gives the class/source 'from/of the great ones' (i.e. among the nobles). 'کسی' here is indefinite 'anyone'. 'کینه جوی' is a compound: 'کینه' (grudge/hatred) + 'جوی' ('seeking') → 'one who seeks/harbors a grudge'. The whole phrase functions as the object of ندید.
segments.s726-g4: که introduces a relative clause modifying 'کسی' ('who/that'). 'با او' = 'toward/with him'. بروی is the poetic/prefixal form of 'رو' ('to go / to turn (one's face)') and here has the sense 'turn/go toward' (an older/persianized present/subjunctive form). 'اندر آورد' is a compound verbal idea 'bring in / cause to come' (آورد = 'bring'), and 'روی' is 'face'. Together the clause says 'who would, toward him, turn/bring (their) face' — i.e. confront or show hostility to him.
segments.s726-g5: به + دستور پاکیزه is a prepositional phrase 'to the pure (پاکیزه) commander/dastur' (دستور here is a title/office or 'commander'/'chief'). 'یک روز' is a temporal adverbial 'one day', and گفت is the past verb 'said' (here introducing reported speech). The sequence gives 'One day he said to the pure commander...'.
segments.s726-g6: که introduces the content clause (an indirect question). 'اندیشه' = 'the thought/intention'. 'تا کی' = 'until when / how long'. 'بود' is the past copula 'was/would be' used here to mean 'would remain' (classical/historic style). 'در نهفت' = 'hidden inside' (در = 'in', نهفت = 'hidden/concealed'). Together: 'how long would this thought remain hidden within?'.
segments.s726-g7: This noun phrase 'کشنده پدر' = 'the father's killer / the slayer of (my) father' (کشنده = 'killer'). 'هر زمان' is an adverbial 'every time / continually', and 'پیش من' = 'before me / in my presence'. The phrase sets up a recurring image: the killer appears before the speaker again and again.
segments.s726-g8: همی‌بگذرد is a habitual/continuative form (همی- + present) meaning 'continually passes / goes by'. 'چون' here is comparative 'as / as if'. 'بود' is the copula in past/subjunctive sense 'were / was' used poetically to make the comparison. 'خویش' (here split as خوی + ش + من) expresses 'one's own / kin' with the possessive clitic ش and 'من' emphasising 'my own'. Together: 'he continually passes by as though he were my own (kinsman)'.
segments.s726-g9: چوروشن is a temporal adverb (archaic contraction of چو روشن) meaning 'when it was light / at dawn' and comes at the clause-initial position. روانم is the subject with the possessive suffix -م 'my' (روان = 'spirit, soul'). پر از خون بود is the predicate: پر از + noun = 'full of (blood)'; بود is the past copula 'was.' The whole clause means 'At dawn my spirit was full of blood.'
segments.s726-g10: همی before a verb in epic/classical Persian signals continuous or habitual aspect (often used to render past ongoing action). پادشاهی کنم uses کن to mean 'act as / rule' (کنم is 1st-person form); here the verb form functions as past-habitual in narration. چون بود ('چون' = 'as' / 'in the manner that'; بود = past copula) links as a comparative/retrospective clause 'as it was' / 'as before.' Together: 'I was still ruling, as it had been.'
segments.s726-g11: نهادند is 3rd-person plural past 'they placed / they set' with خوان (object) 'a banquet.' و is the coordinating conjunction 'and.' می‌چند is a present/imperfective narrative form meaning 'arrange / set (out)' (the story-style imperfective with می-), and خورد is the past 'ate' (here coordinating actions of laying out the feast and eating). The clause describes placing the table and the eating that followed.
segments.s726-g12: هم آن روز is an adverbial phrase 'that very day / on that same day' (هم = 'same/also', آن روز = 'that day'). بندوی را بند کرد is object (بندوی 'bondman') + را (definite object marker; here written joined as رابند) + بند کرد 'bound / tied up' (کرد past 'did / made'). The clause means 'he bound the bondman on that day.'
segments.s726-g13: ازان پس is a fixed sequence meaning 'after that / thereupon.' چنین گفت is 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus, so', گفت = 'said' — the verb is the past-report verb). با رهنما uses با with a person meaning 'to / with the guide' (addressing the guide). The whole segment sets up reported speech to the guide.
segments.s726-g14: که introduces the content clause ('that' / 'ordering that ...'). او را = object 'him' with را marking the definite object. هم‌اکنون is the adverb 'right now / immediately.' ببردست وپا (classical/poetic surface here for the command 'ببند دست و پا') functions as the verbal command in reported speech 'bind hand and foot' — an imperative/subjunctive idea reported after گفت. The clause thus conveys an immediate order to bind him hand and foot.
segments.s726-g15: بریدند is 3rd-person plural past 'they cut' (in epic diction often used for slaying). هم در زمان او is temporal: هم 'also / then', در زمان او 'at his time / at that moment.' بمرد is a past-subjunctive/jussive-form used in narrative to mean 'he died' (a short, poetic way to state the death). The clause narrates that they struck him and he died at that time.
segments.s726-g16: پر از خون is the adjectival phrase 'full of blood' (پر = 'full (of)'), روانش 'his spirit' (روان + possessive -ش), به خسرو 'to Khosrow' (recipient marked by به), and سپرد is the past verb 'handed over / entrusted.' The clause says they handed over his blood-filled spirit to Khosrow (a poetic way of saying he was delivered/dead and given over).

sentences.s726-s1: After that the king sat in peace; when Bahram rose, a warrior came from the road.
sentences.s726-s2: He saw no one among the great who nursed a grudge or who would confront him.
sentences.s726-s3: One day he said to the pure/loyal commander: 'How long will this thought remain hidden within?'
sentences.s726-s4: The slayer of my father continually passes before me, as if he were my own (kinsman).
sentences.s726-s5: At dawn my spirit was full of blood; I was still ruling as it had been.
sentences.s726-s6: They set a banquet and arranged things to eat; and that same day he bound the bondman.
sentences.s726-s7: After that he thus said to the guide: 'Bind him at once, hand and foot.'
sentences.s726-s8: They cut him down, and at that time he died; they delivered his blood‑filled spirit to Khosrow.
